heading: MODULO SETTIMO
card_summary: Chunk 114 presents the seventh module of Zorzi's third Canticle, treating the hierarchical partition of the celestial city and its inhabitants. Drawing on Revelation (John), Ezekiel, Isaiah, Paul, and Gregory the Great, Zorzi maps the ranked seats of the blessed: the supreme throne of God (with Christ seated at the Father's right, the seven spirits, and the Queen/Mary), the twelve apostolic thrones, the twenty-four elders combining the twelve apostles with the twelve patriarchs, the prophets (four major and twelve minor), and the four evangelists figured as the four living creatures with six seraphic wings. The section then turns to the Hebrew Merkavah tradition — drawing on the Sefer Yetzirah attributed to Abraham — and explains the Ofannim, Seraphim, angels of service, and the four holy animals (lion, ox, eagle, man) as the archetypal kings of their respective animal kingdoms, whose ideal images are stored in God's celestial court as seen by Daniel, Ezekiel, and John.
essay:
## Overview and Central Argument

Chunk 114 constitutes the opening sections (1–3) of the seventh module of the third Canticle in Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. The central argument is cosmological-eschatological: the heavenly city is not an undifferentiated unity but a harmoniously ordered hierarchy of dwelling-places (*mansiones*) and thrones (*sedilia*), each corresponding to a rank of sanctity. This hierarchy mirrors — and grounds — the ordered plurality visible in the created world below, since (in Neoplatonic fashion) lower realities are emanations of higher ones. The section moves from the general principle of celestial partition to a detailed mapping of the hierarchy, and then pivots to the Hebrew technical tradition of the Merkavah as a parallel and complementary account.

## Section 1: The Principle of Partition

Zorzi opens by appealing to John (Revelation) and Ezekiel, who "in modo palese" describe the celestial city as divided into foundations, gates, piazzas, fields, temple, and chambers. Paul's list in Ephesians (apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, doctors — for "il perfezionamento dei santi") furnishes the ecclesial ranking, and Christ's own words — "Nella casa del Padre mio ci sono molte dimore" (John 14:2) — authorize the multiplicity of seats. Zorzi is careful to observe that Christ himself declined to specify for whom the individual dwellings are prepared, leaving their assignment to the Father; accordingly, Zorzi promises only to report what prophets and apostles have actually revealed.

The metaphysical principle is stated explicitly: because this lower world displays a manifold partition of seats and lodgings for its inhabitants, the heavenly kingdom — of which earthly realities are *emanazione* — must likewise be so ordered. This is a classic Neoplatonic argument from analogical correspondence between higher and lower.

## Section 2: The Hierarchy of Blessed Seats

The ascending (or rather descending from God) hierarchy Zorzi establishes runs as follows:

**The Supreme Throne.** Isaiah's "Il cielo è il mio trono" (Is. 66:1) identifies the entire heaven as God's seat. Before it stand seven spirits (Rev. 1:4). Christ too is enthroned here (Rev. 3:21; Is. 6:1), and Paul, Stephen, and John attest to this.

**The Queen.** Before even the apostles, Zorzi places the Queen and Empress of all — the Woman of Revelation 12 (clothed with the sun, crowned with twelve stars, moon beneath her feet), identified with the Virgin Mary seated at God's right hand via Psalm 44 (45):10 ("Alla tua destra siede la regina coperta d'oro, con una veste variopinta"). The moon beneath her feet is glossed as "la sinagoga, oppure tutta l'accolta degli eletti" — the synagogue or the whole assembly of the elect — since she is exalted above all creatures and "rivestita di sole, cioè di Cristo."

**The Twenty-Four Elders.** The twelve apostolic thrones (Matt. 19:28: "Siederete su dodici troni per giudicare le dodici tribù di Israele") are matched with the twelve patriarchs of the "stirpe figurale," yielding the twenty-four elders of Revelation 4:4, who worship the living God with antiphonal hymns:

> Et supra thronos, viginti quatuor seniores sedentes circumamicti vestimentis albis, et in capitibus eorum vidi coronas aureas. Qui omnes mutuis vocibus laudabant viventem in saecula saeculorum, atque dicebant: Dignus es Domine Deus noster accipere gloriam, et honorem, et virtutem, quia tu creasti omnia.

<cite>p. 2403 (Latin columnar text)</cite>

**The Prophets.** Following the apostles and patriarchal founders, the prophets hold a sublimer place than ordinary saints but below the apostolic order. The four major prophets (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Daniel), the twelve minor prophets, and many others recorded in the four books of Kings constitute what the Hebrews call the *neviim rishonim* (Propheti anteriores — "Profeti anteriori"), beginning with Samuel and including Nathan, Ahijah, Elijah, Elisha, and the unnamed "sons of the prophets."

**The Evangelists as the Four Living Creatures.** The evangelists, chosen to share in the divine secrets in the order Paul teaches, are prefigured — following Gregory the Great and many others — by the four living creatures of Revelation 4:6–8, "pieni d'occhi" and equipped with six seraphic wings. Their eyes denote illumination by divine light enabling their Gospels to be "perfettamente consonanti con la legge, tanto che non c'è un solo apice o uno iota nella legge che non trovi la propria corrispondenza, in modo mirabile, nel vangelo." The six wings are glossed via Ephesians 3:18 — the six spatial directions of length, width, height, and depth:

> Ut possitis comprehendere cum omnibus sanctis (potissime cum evangelistis, qui plene scripserunt ea, quae ad sanctitatem, et vitam perfectam pertinent) quae sit longitudo, latitudo, sublimitas atque profundum.

<cite>p. 2404 (Latin columnar text)</cite>

Morally, the six directions are the six dimensions of evangelical proclamation; naturally, they correspond to the seraphim's warming power extending through all space like solar heat. John does not list further seats, though Dionysus the Areopagite (Pseudo-Dionysius) reveals many angelic orders treated earlier in the work.

## Section 3: The Merkavah — Hebrew Throne-Mysticism

The third section introduces the Hebrew technical term *Merkavah* (merkavah, "trono di Dio secondo gli ebrei"), drawn from Ezekiel and elaborated by the rabbinic doctors, "soprattutto nel Libro della formazione, attribuito ad Abramo" — i.e., the *Sefer Yetzirah*. Zorzi acknowledges that Latin readers will find these terms unfamiliar but insists they are not "inutili" precisely because they are not trivial:

> Nulli dubium, videbuntur extranea ne dicam inepta, quia nihil triviale penitus sapiunt.

<cite>p. 2406 (Latin columnar text)</cite>

The four components of the Merkavah are:

- **Ofannim** (*ophaninos*): angels charged with disposing matter so that form may be introduced, since matter has no active power of its own.
- **Serafini** (*seraphinos*): "potenze riscaldanti" dispensing life and form.
- **Angeli del servizio** (*angeli ministerii*): multiple in kind, corresponding to their many ministries.
- **Animali santi** (*animalia sanctitatis*): the four holy animals of Ezekiel 1 and Revelation 4 — lion, ox (vitello/bue), eagle, man.

## The Four Royal Animals as Platonic Ideas

Zorzi offers an anagogical reading of the four living creatures (following the "antichi padri" against the allegorical/moral readings catalogued by Jerome on Ezekiel). They are the "re dei quattro generi animali": the ox is king among domestic animals, the lion among wild beasts, the eagle among birds, and man among all rational beings. Man holds primacy above the angels in both sexes: in the masculine, Christ surpasses them (Heb. 1:4: "di tanto superiore agli angeli di quanto il nome che ha ereditato è più eccellente del loro"); in the feminine, Mary is "esaltata sopra tutti i cori degli angeli."

The deeper question — why are lion, ox, and eagle present at God's throne? — receives a Platonic-idealist answer. Since all things emanate from the divine, all things must have their image and idea (*imaginem et ideam*) in the divine court, inscribed in the "libro dei conti di Dio sommo." Zorzi cites Jerome's commentary on Psalm 138 (139):15-16 to establish that God knows even the elements composing man before formation, counts the stars by name, numbers the grains of sand and drops of rain, holding in himself the "idea" of all these things. Therefore the four royal animals in the Merkavah are:

> tamquam imagines, et ideae singulares eorum, quae hic sunt, sicut et singularium regnorum et principatuum apud Imperatorem habentur insignia.

<cite>p. 2409 (Latin columnar text)</cite>

The animals, horses, serpents, dragons, leopards, lions, oxen, and various beasts reported by John, Daniel, and Ezekiel from their visions of the celestial court are thus understood as Platonic archetypes — singular ideas of earthly genera held in the divine mind, much as an emperor keeps the insignia of all his subordinate kingdoms.

## Technical Vocabulary and Sources

Key technical terms introduced or deployed: *mansiones* (dwelling-places), *sedilia/thrones* (seats), *Mercava/merkavah* (the divine chariot-throne), *ophaninos* (Ofannim), *seraphinos* (Seraphim), *animalia sanctitatis* (the four holy creatures), *imagines et ideae singulares* (singular archetypical images/ideas).

Primary scriptural sources: Isaiah 66:1; 6:1; Psalm 44(45):10; John 14:2; Matt. 19:28; Revelation 1:4; 3:21; 4:4–8; 12:1; Ephesians 4:11–12; 3:18; Hebrews 1:4; Psalm 138(139):15–16.

Patristic and classical authorities: Gregory the Great (four living creatures as figures of evangelists), Jerome (*In Ezechielem*; commentary on Ps. 138), Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (angelic orders, cited by reference to earlier treatment), Paul (ordering of ecclesiastical grades).

Kabbalistic sources: Sefer Yetzirah (*Libro della formazione*, attributed to Abraham) as the principal rabbinic authority for the Merkavah and its four components.

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk exemplifies the synthetic method of *De Harmonia Mundi*: the architecture of the heavenly city is not merely a matter of eschatological speculation but is a structural mirror of the harmonic ordering of all creation. The celestial partition is simultaneously Neoplatonic (emanation, archetypal ideas), scriptural (John, Ezekiel, Isaiah, Paul), kabbalistic (Merkavah, Ofannim, Serafini), and patristic (Gregory, Jerome, Dionysius). The convergence of these traditions is itself the demonstration of universal harmonia. The chapter closes by deferring to a treatment of the "ospiti" (the inhabitants) as the next means by which the celestial partition will be better understood.
